item.content:encoded:
woooooooo my first skip finally cracked 100!

Thanks for reading this, everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Grandma who, caring for his grandson, put a deadly disgusting curse that guarantees slow and kill which can only be avoided by totally removing your scalp

How lovely. +1

PS. How the F!@# did you think of this?
PS2. I already read the 'warning spoiler' but seriously, how, the F!@#, did you think of this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap.

Yes, definitely a +1. The sudden escalation blindsides you and is both sickening and very disturbing. The fact that it comes from a letter from a grandmother makes it so much worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow…

That's really strange and disturbing. I dig the subtle simplicity of this piece.

+1 from me, good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuckkkkk. Honestly tried to upvote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus, I didn't even think about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCPs ain't always gotta be big or super weird. Sometimes you need the small ones that make you think. I wasn't particularly creeped out by it, but I see others are, and I like the concept and the execution couldn't be better. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's some solid fridge horror right there.

I have to upvote for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Added a click-to-enlarge feature. I couldn't figure out how to get it to open in a new tab, but whatever.

Anyways, upvoted because the method of death is really squicky, and it's nice to see the letter contents in their proper place, rather than at the end the document as some sort of stinger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is creepier than it has any right to be. Very old school SCP in feel. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I described my intentions with this piece on the third page of this forum. I was shooting for body horror, but there was an additional theme I was going for. You can look at the post if you're interested.

Anyway, you or anyone else has my permission to edit the page and add a "click to enlarge" feature. I've spent a pretty long time trying to figure out how to do this, so I am deferring to people with more syntax experience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was pretty ambivalent on this piece but after reading this comment I am giving this a solid +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, that's the genius in this piece. That's why it makes sense. That's why it's hundreds of little, individually inconsequential things burrowing into you.

item.title: This letter puts you in a rather hairy situation...
(no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I wrote this article, I was going for something a little more than body horror. Sure, that was the main effect I wanted, but the overall theme I was trying to express is passive-aggression.

+ WARNING SPOILERS
- I mean, if you're reading the third page of the discussion forum before reading the article itself.  But hey, I'm a collapsible text message, not a cop.

For me, passive-aggression is much worse than simple aggression. Dealing with a passive-aggressive person can be exhausting as your patience slowly whittles away, but their insults are even more insulting because they have the gall to pretend their comments are innocuous. Trying to make progress in an argument with one is painfully slow.

And that's what we see in the letter. Davey's grandma is not only nagging him for the umpteenth time about leaving the area to attend college (despite it being a done deal) as she "praises" him for studying, but she passively insults all his friends at the college he goes to (implying he is bad at keeping good company) and passively insults his long hair repeatedly.

There are plenty of SCPs that are just things that kill you in a gross way. This isn't the first one to kill the person it affects slowly, but the lethality of it is passive-aggressive as well. If you thought this thing was just a normal letter and read it aloud, you wouldn't notice anything right away. You probably wouldn't notice anything at all for a few weeks. But eventually, you would notice that your hair was getting shorter on your head. And for some reason, the hair around your body is getting shorter too. And now there's this weird itch under your skin. It's not that bad at first, just a mild irritation, but it steadily gets worse and worse. By the time you're losing control over your muscles and the ability to move, you're in incredible pain. But you're still alive. Hair is growing through your bladder making you incontinent and has shredded a certain sensitive area if you're a guy, but you're still alive. Eventually, if you had a very hairy chest, you probably suffocate on your hair, else your head hair minces your brain.

But it took an awful long time to kill you. The thing killing you only progresses as quickly as your hair normally grows. If you've gotten a bad haircut before and waited for everything to grow back and look normal, you know that hair growth is painfully slow.

And just like aggression that's intended to come across as innocuous, I doubt you ever put it together that the thing killing you was just this stupid letter.

That's the joke. Lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel this needs a "click to enlarge"

This would be nice. I found myself thinking the same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The grandma mentioning gender is easy. It's 1970. Girls have long hair, boys have short hair. If Davey didn't get his hair cut like a good boy, he'd look like a girl.

But maybe Davey wants to look like a girl. Who knows kids these days?

That's all. It's all about the hair.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found it interesting and amusing, but I feel like you didn't go far enough. You didn't need much more, but a little more detail or possible intrigue in the effect could've pushed this over the top for me. But as it stands, it's an amusing piece as you're reading it but not something that's going to stick with me once I'm finished reading it. Downvoted for now but would upvote if it had a little more meat to it.

I'm not sure what else it needs, perhaps just some more details on the exact mechanics of how the hair grows, and the endgame if you just let the person become hair-infused. Maybe they turn into cousin it or something. The fixation on the grandma commenting on gender also has no payoff, and while I don't think that element should be entirely removed, maybe cutting one of the references in the letter would make it less odd that it isn't followed-up on later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree, but that's mainly because it makes for a great literary device.

Check out SCP-089. When do you feel the true horror of that piece? It's positively best that the body horror is at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've noticed something interesting. If the whole body horror sequence came before the note, I feel like this wouldn't be doing so well on the mainsite. Does anyone else think that too? Feel free to disagree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A real series I feel to this, and in a good way. Short, simple, to the point, creepy. Well done. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't a fan of this (still not a fan), but the fact that you implicitly give the reader some subtle background concerning the grandmother with the effect makes me no-vote. That's really clever in an article about body horror.

I personally think body horror needs to go all-out to be effective. I see enough weird monsters and designs in various movies or games or pictures drawn or whatever (I even find some of them cute but I digress) that simple body horror doesn't do it for me. Maybe it's desensitization? But this is my taste in fiction concerning that, and I either need visuals to go along with it (like in horror movies/games/manga), or the imagery has to be strong enough to hold me. This doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is, in fact, no accounting for taste. My sister insists the only taste I have is in my mouth, but I still associate with her, so that's clearly not the case.

2) Understood and I agree making the image click-to-enlarge would be beneficial, especially since this one is shrunk down so much in the page.
5) That this does only happen with this one letter is what makes the anomaly and raises a lot of questions, which kinda helps it, in my mind. What is it about this letter or the person who wrote it that caused this to happen. It sends the mind racing off down the paths of possibility.

There's a very, very fine line between lack of development and being just developed enough. The general perception is definitely leaning toward this one falling on the positive side and the zombabysite on the negative. If I had to guess, I would say that this one leaves SO much unexplained, that it doesn't break suspension of disbelief and causes more questions than it answers. The zombabysite, on the other hand, attempted to answer all the questions regarding it, which hampered the willing suspension of disbelief and ultimately lead to it failing.

Then again, it may just come down to the tastes of the people voting on it that day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the explanation!

1) Yes I phrased that badly, I meant that "only if you read the original directly".
2) I don't think we can assume that there aren't clues to a skip in the image, several articles do have them. I tend to read these on my phone when I have a few minutes so not having it click to enlarge was frustrating. Maybe that is just me.
5) That seems rather thin and specious to me. Presumably this happens all the time and isn't replicated. My own grandmother has expressed this desire in regards to some of my cousins in the past, and none of them have this condition.

Doesn't have to be science, but I think I prefer skips that have a bit of onion layers to them and that I need to think about in order to understand what is going on.

It does seem weird to me that say that undead baby parasite one failed for being a weird gross thing with little depth but this one is even less developed and it is successful. De gustibus non est disputatum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Point the First: I think this may be where you're missing the hook. You read the letter aloud and then the effect manifests. You don't need to remain in contact with the letter afterward.

Point the Second: Right click image, open in new tab. This should work for everything except mobile. Agreed that a click to enlarge would help, but unnecessary, as the text is reproduced in its entirety in the text of the article.

Point the Third: It's footnoted that the causal mechanism isn't understood and is being researched. It's a bit of handwavium, but the actual mechanism isn't the focus.

Point the Fourth: Not that I can see, either explicitly or implicitly, but then, the how isn't the focus, it's the what.

Point the Fifth: Gramma misses her grandson and wishes he'd cut his hair. Apparently, she wished that really, really hard when she wrote this letter.

The focus of this article isn't the how of what it does, but purely what it does, which is gross and squicky and awful. For my reading, it hearkens back to some of the best of Series I, in that it causes an immediate and visceral reaction of first 'ugh!' and then 'holy fridge horror!' as the realisation of just how terrifying and painful a death having your body hair grow inwards. It may well just not be your cuppa. Judging by your own articles and comments, you seem to prefer the harder science aspects of scips where actual science is being twisted into pretzel shapes. This just isn't that kind of scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Weird thing with a lethal effect" isn't necessarily a bad thing; in fact, that's pretty much the entire concept which the wiki revolves around (or did once, anyway).

It's all about execution, and most people seem to like how this one was done. I somewhat like it myself; well written, not bloated up with irrelevant or narmy details, novel killing method. Could be a lot worse, especially for a first submission!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay I reread this and I still don't get it.

  • You read the letter out loud and then hair grows through your body, killing you gross, but only if you are holding the original letter.
  • I needed to zoom to view the image well enough to comfortably read it (again I feel this needs a "click to enlarge").
  • The cause, mechanism, or parameters of this phenomenon are not directly addressed.
  • Are there hints as what is causing this, that I am missing?
  • Is there some subtle narrative here that I am missing?

I just don't see how this isn't just a weird thing with a lethal effect (cursed magic item) and we down vote these into oblivion everyday. What is special here that I am missing?

I guess I am going to reluctantly downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad to see this is doing well, it's a great combination of simplicity and good execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Ew ew ew ew ew. Honestly, I've read plenty of pages that give me a "stomach" kind of gross-out, but this is the first I've read that gives me a proper "skin-crawling" gross-out. (Then again, I haven't been around long.) It also makes me think that maybe this'll be the way to go if I ever get around to writing a page. Short, clipped, hella creepy. All of the kudos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm headcanoning grandma as a mildly senile magic user of some sort trying to give Davey (actual name Danny) a haircut.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disgusted. Unnerved. And that special kind of skin-crawl. +1 and looking forward to more from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get it, but the section on the effect is worded weirdly. I had to stop and reread one particular line several times.

Once there is no more hair above a particular follicle, the follicle will begin contributing cells to the inward-growing hair.

I never made sense of it on the first read through. I just skipped and read on, but the time it took for me to read that line 4 times completely broke the slight tension of the piece. Maybe rewording it might help?

No-vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The body horror here didn't particularly grip me, unfortunately, and that aside there's little to appreciate. However, it's clearly effective for a lot of people, so congratulations on finding a suitably unnerving and novel strand of body horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, you would have to be close enough to the original letter to be able to read the words written on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm confused about something. It says it has to be read aloud for her effect to work, but reading copies won't trigger it, does that mean you have to be in physical proximity to the original letter and read it out loud for it to work? Because if not, this falls into the same trap as the "it's behind you!" SCP that was deleted for being easy to disprove.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing similar sentiments… and i did downvote initially, until I ended up with neutral. The article speaks for itself (which is appreciative), but the subject-matter ain't my cup of tea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I seem to be missing whatever everyone else is getting here. It just seems like a weird letter that kills you in a gross way to me.

Also it was a bit frustrating to have to zoom the letter and then discover later that there is a full transcript that appears (unless I missed something) to be identical. Maybe put a click to enlarge on the image?

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my new favourites, god damn was this one spine tingling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job, brew.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck. This is not what you'd expect from a letter from your grandmother. Like, holy fuck. And the brevity only helps. This is good for a first skip, and good for a body horror skip. Good job, and welcome to the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now THAT'S what I mean by Series III depth, Series I feel! Short, to the point, nothing extraneous, you know the story, and the effect is gruesome, yet makes sense!

+1. Easy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Just… wow. My only nitpick about this is there's a single addendum, so why label it with a number?

Upvoted nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made the skin on my scalp crawl. I'm trying not to think about where else I grow hair.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that was unexpected.

item.title: Thank you!
item.content:encoded:
This is my first attempt at posting something to the main site. Here we go…

I'd like to thank Wobblebug, EldritchCyanide, and Djoric for their feedback on my draft. I'd also like to thank CupertinoEffect and Aelanna for their feedback on stuff I've tried before in the sandbox.
